title: CAP Theorem
summary: CAP theorem with concrete examples, CP vs AP systems, network partitions in the real world, and PACELC extension.

sections.content:
## The CAP Theorem

Proven by Eric Brewer (2000) and formally by Gilbert & Lynch (2002): a distributed data store can only **guarantee two of three** properties simultaneously:

- **C — Consistency**: Every read receives the most recent write or an error
- **A — Availability**: Every request receives a (non-error) response — no guarantee it's the most recent data
- **P — Partition Tolerance**: The system continues operating even when network messages are dropped or delayed

**The key insight**: In any real distributed system, network partitions WILL happen. So the real choice is **CP vs AP** — you can't avoid P.

sections.title: CAP Theorem Triangle
sections.content:
graph TD
    C[Consistency\nAll nodes see same data] --- A[Availability\nSystem always responds]
    A --- P[Partition Tolerance\nSurvives network splits]
    P --- C
    CP[CP Systems\nPostgreSQL, ZooKeeper, HBase\nChoose consistency over availability]
    AP[AP Systems\nCassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB\nChoose availability over consistency]
    CA[CA Systems\nOnly possible without partitions\nSingle-node databases]
    C & P --> CP
    A & P --> AP
    C & A --> CA
    style CP fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style AP fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style CA fill:#6b7280,color:#fff

## CP Systems — Consistency over Availability

During a partition, a CP system **refuses to respond** rather than return potentially stale data.

**Example: ZooKeeper**
- Used for distributed coordination (leader election, locks)
- If a majority quorum is not reachable → ZooKeeper refuses reads and writes
- Guarantees every client sees the same, consistent view

**Example: PostgreSQL with synchronous replication**
- Write is only acknowledged when replica has persisted it
- If replica is unreachable → write blocks (CP behavior)

**When to use CP**: financial transactions, configuration management, leader election — where stale data causes serious problems.

## AP Systems — Availability over Consistency

During a partition, an AP system **continues serving requests** even if different nodes return different data.

**Example: Cassandra**
- With quorum writes, if some replicas are unreachable, write still succeeds to available replicas
- Reads may return stale data temporarily
- Eventually, when partition heals, replicas sync via gossip + hinted handoff

**Example: DNS**
- DNS changes propagate with TTL delay — different servers return different IPs temporarily
- Always returns an answer (high availability) even if not the latest one

**When to use AP**: shopping carts, social media likes, analytics, user preferences — where slight staleness is acceptable.

sections.title: Network Partition — CP vs AP Behavior
sections.content:
graph TB
    subgraph Network Partition
        N1[Node 1] -.-|partition!| N2[Node 2]
    end
    subgraph CP System Behavior
        CP1[Node 1] -->|refuse request| CP_E[503 Service Unavailable]
        CP1 -.->|cannot reach Node 2| CP_E
    end
    subgraph AP System Behavior
        AP1[Node 1] -->|serve stale data| AP_R[200 OK maybe stale]
    end
    style CP_E fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style AP_R fill:#d97706,color:#fff

## PACELC — The Real-World Extension

CAP only addresses partition scenarios. **PACELC** (Abadi, 2012) extends this:

> If there is a Partition (P): choose between Availability (A) and Consistency (C)
> Else (E, no partition): choose between Latency (L) and Consistency (C)

This captures the everyday trade-off: even without partitions, you trade off latency vs consistency.

| System | Partition | Normal Operation |
|---|---|---|
| PostgreSQL (sync replication) | CP | EC (higher latency for consistency) |
| Cassandra | AP | EL (low latency, eventual consistency) |
| DynamoDB (eventual) | AP | EL |
| DynamoDB (strong) | CP | EC |

sections.title: CAP is often misunderstood
sections.content: CAP doesn't mean you pick two properties and completely abandon the third. Real systems fine-tune their trade-offs: Cassandra lets you configure consistency level per operation (ONE, QUORUM, ALL). You can choose stronger consistency for critical reads and weaker for analytics.

sections.question: In a distributed system, why can you not avoid the P (Partition Tolerance) requirement?
options: Because all distributed systems use eventually consistent algorithms
Because network partitions (dropped/delayed messages) are inevitable in any real distributed network
Because partitions are required for horizontal scaling
Because the CAP theorem was proven mathematically to require P
sections.explanation: Network partitions — where nodes cannot communicate with each other — happen in every real distributed system due to hardware failures, network issues, or timeouts. Since you cannot prevent partitions, you must tolerate them (P is non-negotiable), making the real choice between consistency (C) and availability (A) during a partition.
